start_cue: One item at a time, ordered by cost and reversibility. The foundations — body composition, skin, hair, grooming — do far more than anything in this category.

weeks.detail: Basic nail kit (clippers, file, cuticle tool — under $15) and a hand cream. Trim, file smooth, moisturize on Sunday evening. Repeat weekly. Within two weeks the hands read meaningfully cleaner in close interaction — handshakes, gestures, anywhere hands are visible.

Roughly half an ounce to 0.7 ounces of water per pound of bodyweight daily. A water bottle at your desk refilled a set number of times — no tracking app needed. Tier-3 effort; stacks with everything else at effectively zero cost.

One optical-shop appointment. Ask the optician for frames that oppose your face shape rather than echo it — angular frames for a round face, softer rounded frames for an angular or square face. Try five to seven pairs, photograph each, compare, choose. If you don't wear glasses, skip this step entirely — the watch decision moves up.

One clean minimal analog watch, metal or leather strap, unfussy face, worn daily. Entry under $200; mid-range $200–800; high-end above. A $300 watch worn every day outperforms a $3,000 watch worn for events. Buy the one that will actually sit on your wrist through a workday.

At this point the foundations are running. Add at most one additional item per quarter — a thin chain if the neck and collarbone are lean enough, a simple ring if the hands read well, eye-whitening drops for specific contexts. Evaluate each addition after a month against: is this making the presentation read more intentional, or cluttered?

graduation: Nails, hydration, glasses (if applicable), and watch all running automatically is the graduation signal. Further additions should be quarterly at most. Reassess the overall presentation quarterly rather than weekly — quiet layering only reads as intentional when each layer has had time to settle.
